instructions: Drag and drop the words into the correct order to form a grammatically correct sentence. One word may be extra and should not be used.

questions.context: You just told your friend you missed your morning alarm.
questions.prompt: What did your friend suggest you do?
questions.fullSentence: She suggested that I should set two alarms tomorrow.

questions.context: You are talking about a new policy at your university.
questions.prompt: What does your classmate want to know?
questions.fullSentence: When will the new policy take effect?

questions.context: You are discussing a group project with a teammate.
questions.prompt: What did your professor remind the class about?
questions.fullSentence: The professor reminded us that the report must be submitted by Friday midnight.

questions.context: You ran into a neighbor who is curious about your studies.
questions.prompt: What did your neighbor ask you?
questions.fullSentence: What are you planning to major in?

questions.context: A friend is asking about your weekend plans.
questions.prompt: What would you like to do if the weather is nice?
questions.fullSentence: I was thinking we could go for a hike in the park.

questions.context: Your roommate is complaining about noise in the library.
questions.fullSentence: The librarian announced that the quiet study zone would be enforced more strictly.

questions.context: You are talking to your advisor about your schedule.
questions.prompt: What did your advisor ask you to consider?
questions.fixedStart: My advisor asked me to consider
questions.fullSentence: My advisor asked me to consider taking fewer courses next semester to balance my workload.

questions.context: You are explaining why you were late to a meeting.
questions.prompt: What did you tell your manager?
questions.fullSentence: I apologized and explained that the train had been delayed by over thirty minutes.

questions.context: You are discussing a scholarship with a friend.
questions.prompt: What does your friend want to know about the deadline?
questions.fullSentence: Do you know when the scholarship applications are due?

questions.context: You are helping a new student find their classroom.
questions.prompt: What did the new student ask you?
questions.fullSentence: Could you tell me where the science building is located?

questions.context: You are writing a message to your coworker about a meeting.
questions.explanation: 'Rescheduled' already means moved to another time, so 'postponed' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are telling your roommate about a package delivery.
questions.explanation: The correct word order in a 'while' clause is subject + verb: 'while you were out'. The misplaced 'were' before 'you' is extra.

questions.context: You are explaining why you are late to class.
questions.explanation: 'Late' is the correct adverb here. 'Lately' means recently, which doesn't fit the context.

questions.context: You are asking your professor for an extension.
questions.explanation: 'By next Monday' is the correct phrase. 'Next' appears once — the extra is the duplicate placement. Correct order: submit the assignment by next Monday.

questions.context: You are describing your weekend plans to a friend.
questions.explanation: 'Planning' already expresses the plan. Using 'plan' again creates redundancy, so it is the extra word.

questions.context: You are describing a rule at your workplace.
questions.explanation: The idiom is 'at all times', not 'at every times'. 'Every' is the extra word.

questions.context: You are leaving a note for your neighbor.
questions.explanation: 'Leave' is the correct verb here. 'Go' creates a repetitive meaning and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing an email to a hotel to confirm your reservation.
questions.explanation: The sentence is about confirming the stay duration, not the room type. 'Double' is not needed here and is the extra word.

questions.context: A friend is asking for directions to the library.
questions.explanation: The expression 'on your right' is complete. Adding 'side' is redundant in this context.

questions.context: You are telling a classmate about a canceled lecture.
questions.explanation: 'Illness' already communicates the reason. 'Sick' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are describing what you did over the holiday.
questions.explanation: 'With our family' implies togetherness. Adding 'together' is repetitive and it is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing a notice to students about the cafeteria.
questions.explanation: 'Renovation' specifies the reason for closure. 'Construction' overlaps in meaning and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are texting a friend about a surprise party.
questions.explanation: A 'surprise' party is by definition secret. Using 'secret' is redundant; it is the extra word.

questions.context: You are describing your study routine to a friend.
questions.explanation: 'Right after' already establishes the time. 'Before' contradicts it and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing a complaint about a noisy neighbor.
questions.explanation: Both 'difficult' and 'hard' mean the same thing here. Only one is needed; 'hard' is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing a thank-you note to your host family.
questions.explanation: 'Truly grateful' and 'warm welcome' already express appreciation. 'Kindly' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are advising a new student about campus life.
questions.explanation: 'Get familiar with' is the correct phrase here. 'Accustomed' would require a different structure and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are describing a new store policy to a customer.
questions.explanation: 'Receipt' already serves as proof of purchase. Adding 'proof' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are messaging your doctor's office about an appointment.
questions.explanation: 'Reschedule' already implies the appointment exists. 'Scheduled' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are reporting a problem with your internet service.
questions.explanation: 'Disconnecting' clearly describes the problem. 'Dropping' overlaps in meaning and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing to a friend about moving to a new apartment.
questions.explanation: 'Within my budget' already means it is affordable. 'Affordable' is redundant and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are leaving a message for a colleague about a report.
questions.explanation: 'As soon as you finish revising it' is a time clause. 'Possible' belongs to a different phrase and is the extra word.

questions.context: You are telling your friend about a book you recommend.
questions.explanation: 'Manage my finances' is the correct phrase. 'Control' means something similar and is redundant — it is the extra word.

questions.context: You are explaining why you are applying for a scholarship.
questions.explanation: 'Financial difficulties' already refers to money-related problems. Adding 'money' is redundant; it is the extra word.

questions.context: You are writing a message to a gym to pause your membership.
questions.explanation: 'Put my membership on hold' is the standard idiom for pausing a service. 'Pause' is the extra word.

tips: Read all the words first before placing any — get a sense of the sentence structure.
Look for the subject and main verb first — these anchor the sentence.
Watch out for words that seem to fit but are synonyms of another word already in the list.
Connectors like 'because', 'when', 'while', 'which', and 'if' signal a clause — place them carefully.
After placing all words, read the sentence aloud in your head — if it sounds awkward, one word may be in the wrong position.
Common extra words: synonyms (hard/difficult), redundant descriptors (secret surprise), and duplicated verbs (go/leave).
whatToExpect: You will be given 5–7 words to arrange into one correct sentence.
One word will always be extra — it must not be included in your answer.
Sentences come from everyday situations: emails, texts, notices, and conversations.
Grammar focus: word order, subject-verb agreement, connectors, redundancy.
Questions get slightly harder as the set progresses.
